title: Carved cherry serving spoon
subtitle: A wide-bowled serving spoon from green fruitwood, axe-roughed and hook-knife hollowed
excerpt: A serving spoon asks for a wider, deeper bowl than an eating spoon and rewards the carver who has already made one smaller spoon from green wood. Cherry is a close-grained fruitwood that carves cleanly when green and finishes to a warm red-brown that deepens with use and oiling. A second spoon-carving project for building confidence with the hook knife.
sourceNotes: Fruitwood spoon and serving-implement forms documented in Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) and in British museum collections of domestic treen.

glossaryTerms.definition: Freshly felled wood still high in moisture content. Cherry worked green is considerably softer than the seasoned equivalent and less likely to split during axe roughing.
A short section of branch or trunk prepared for carving. The billet for a serving spoon is riven from a larger piece so the grain runs true along the length.
Split along the grain using an axe or froe rather than sawn across it. A riven face follows the grain continuously and produces a stronger blank than a sawn equivalent.
A short curved carving knife ground on the inside of the curve, used to hollow a spoon bowl. Cuts across and with the grain efficiently from any entry angle in the bowl cavity.

projectSchedule.title: Rough out and hollow the bowl
projectSchedule.body: Rive the billet, axe the profile, sloyd the handle and outside of bowl, hook-knife the bowl to depth. One full carving session.

projectSchedule.title: Sand and oil after drying
projectSchedule.body: After a week of drying, take a final light knife pass or sand to 240 grit where needed, then apply raw linseed oil.

projectSchedule.title: Oil cured: food-safe
projectSchedule.body: Two weeks after oiling, the raw linseed oil has cured. The spoon is ready for the table.

content.text: A serving spoon is the natural second spoon project after an eating spoon. The bowl is wider and deeper, the handle longer, and the overall scale puts more sustained demand on the
hook knife
. Cherry is a dense, close-grained fruitwood that responds well to both axe and hook-knife work. It carves cleanly when
green
and finishes to a warm red-brown that deepens with every oil application and in kitchen use.
What you need: wood
A green cherry
billet
, 28 to 30 cm long, split or
riven

from a branch 6 to 8 cm in diameter. Cherry is available from timber yards that stock fruitwood, or from a tree surgeon cutting an orchard tree. A fresh cut is best; if the billet cannot be worked within a week, seal the end grain with PVA to slow drying. Apple and pear are excellent alternatives: both carve cleanly when green and produce a similarly warm finished surface.
What you need: tools

A carving axe; a sharp sloyd knife; a hook knife with a medium curve. A leather strop for all three. A 1000-grit waterstone for the hook knife's inside bevel if the strop alone cannot restore the edge after extended bowl work. Sandpaper to 240 grit is optional; a clean knife finish needs no sanding. Raw linseed oil.
The work
Step 1: Rive and rough the billet

If starting from a round section of branch, rive it down the centre with the axe driven in line with the grain, or set a splitting wedge at one end and drive it through with a mallet. The flat riven face will become the back of the spoon. Mark the spoon profile on the flat face with pencil: a long handle tapering to a wide oval bowl shape. The bowl end should be about 8 to 9 cm wide at the widest point, and the total length 28 to 30 cm.
Step 2: Axe the profile

Axe away waste from both sides of the pencilled profile to produce the spoon outline. Work carefully around the bowl curve, leaving the back of the bowl slightly convex and proud; it will be refined with the sloyd knife. Take light chopping cuts with the axe perpendicular to the grain at the outer sides of the blank, and longer splitting cuts along the handle. Keep all fingers behind the line of cut.
Step 3: Sloyd the handle and outside of bowl
Use
push cuts

along the handle to round it to a comfortable oval section, about 15 to 18 mm in diameter. The bowl back should curve smoothly from flat at the handle junction to a gentle dome at the tip. Check the spoon from all angles and correct any flat spots or ridges. The bowl walls at the rim should be about 5 mm thick before hollowing.
Step 4: Hollow the bowl

Hold the spoon handle against the thigh and brace the bowl against the palm of the off-hand. Begin in the centre of the bowl and work toward the rim in short scooping cuts, rotating the spoon to follow the grain. The finished bowl should be 10 to 12 mm deep at the centre. Work to within 3 to 4 mm of the pencilled rim line. Check wall thickness by pressing from outside with a fingertip: a thin spot will feel flexible.
At the curved sides of the bowl the grain direction shifts as the tool moves around the arc; reverse the cut direction when the surface begins to tear. Use a
stop cut
at the transition between bowl floor and rim to define a clean shoulder before removing material beyond it.
Step 5: Dry before finishing

Leave the carved spoon to dry for seven days in a warm room, away from direct heat or strong sunlight. A paper bag is a useful drying container: it breathes while slowing surface drying to match the core rate and reduces the risk of splits. The spoon will lighten in colour and stiffen noticeably as it dries.
Step 6: Final refinement and sand

After drying, remove any raised grain fibres with a light final knife pass or with 240-grit sandpaper on the outside surfaces. The bowl interior retains its knife-finished texture. Wipe away all dust before oiling.
Finishing

Apply raw linseed oil generously and allow to soak for one hour. Work the oil into the bowl interior with a fingertip. Wipe off all surface oil with a dry cloth. Cure for two weeks before food contact. Cherry continues to darken with each oiling, developing a deep reddish-brown over months of use.
Variations
Carve a smaller companion spoon for serving jam or conserve: the same form at 18 cm total length with a narrower, shallower bowl.
Use apple wood in place of cherry: apple is harder and carves more slowly but produces an extremely dense, fine-grained finished surface.
Add a kolrosing border line around the bowl rim after carving and before oiling for a decorative finish.
Care

Hand wash and dry immediately after use. Never dishwasher, never soak. Re-oil every three to six months or whenever the wood looks dry. The bowl will darken with cooking oils and dressings over time, which is characteristic of a well-used wooden spoon.

items.symptom: The hook knife tears the wood rather than cutting cleanly
items.cause: The knife is cutting against the grain in the bowl, or the edge is dull.
items.fix: Reverse the cut direction. Strop the hook knife on the inside bevel. Cherry grain in a bowl can reverse direction within a few millimetres; always cut toward the high side of the bowl curve.

items.symptom: The spoon splits along the grain during drying
items.cause: Drying was too rapid, or the bowl walls are thinner on one side than the other, causing differential shrinkage.
items.fix: Wrap future spoons in a paper bag to slow drying. Check bowl wall thickness during carving by pressing from outside: it should feel firm and consistent all the way round.

items.symptom: The bowl is noticeably deeper on one side than the other
items.cause: More material was removed from one half of the bowl during hook-knife work.
items.fix: Check bowl depth from the flat back surface by placing a straight edge across the rim and measuring to the floor with a thin skewer. Correct the shallower side with additional hook-knife passes.
